skill.name: skill-marketplace-publisher
skill.description: Publish a Codex or Claude skill to public skill marketplaces by auditing public safety, preparing a clean package, publishing a GitHub repository, and submitting marketplace requests.
skill.summary: Prepare, audit, publish, and submit Codex or Claude skills to public marketplaces.
skill.author: Sliense-ysd

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high and critical patterns, but most are false positives from markdown backticks, marketplace URLs, regex definitions, and words such as SKILL.md. Confirmed risks are intentional: scripts read local skill files and submit user-provided repository or contact details to public marketplace endpoints, so use requires user consent and endpoint verification.

medium_findings.title: Outbound Marketplace Submission Requests
medium_findings.description: The submit script posts repository URLs, notes, optional email addresses, and listing messages to Skillstore and SkillMap endpoints. This is expected for the skill, but it can disclose user-provided project and contact details if run without clear consent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly builds JSON payloads and sends POST requests to fixed external marketplace URLs. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so the risk is disclosure through intended operation rather than hidden exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Recursive Local Skill File Reading
medium_findings.description: The audit script walks a user-supplied skill directory, reads text-like files, and prints detected secret-like lines. This is legitimate audit behavior, but findings may expose sensitive snippets in terminal logs or captured output.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem traversal and output of matching lines are explicit in the script. The code does not send this data over the network, but local output exposure is a real operational concern.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Flagged as Execution
low_findings.description: Many external-command findings occur in fenced documentation examples and marketplace notes. They are not executed by the skill automatically, but users should review commands before running publishing steps such as git, rsync, gh, and Python scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown instructions or URL notes, not runtime shell execution. The only risk is if a user voluntarily runs documented commands without verifying paths and destinations.

low_findings.title: Secret and Environment Access Patterns Are Scanner Definitions
low_findings.description: The reported private-key, API-key, dotenv, and SSH-file hits appear inside regex pattern definitions for the audit scanner. No evidence found that the script reads environment variables, imports dotenv, or contains real secret material.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious strings are compiled regular expressions used to detect secrets in other files. The script imports os for directory walking, but does not access os.environ or load dotenv files as configuration.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alerts point to descriptions, markdown references, argparse setup, or marketplace text. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic operations in the cited files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found user-facing text and parser construction, not cryptographic API calls. The scanner likely matched substrings such as md in SKILL.md or marketplace wording.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Alerts Are Relative Path Examples
low_findings.description: The path traversal findings are caused by documentation examples using relative paths to neighboring local tooling. They are not used in code to open attacker-controlled paths, although users should adjust paths for their own workspace.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The ../ sequences occur in markdown command examples, not in a file-access implementation. The actual audit script resolves the supplied root path and walks that directory intentionally.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network POST to Third-Party Marketplace APIs
dangerous_patterns.description: The script sends JSON over HTTPS to fixed marketplace endpoints. Users must confirm the endpoint and payload before submission.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The outbound POST helper and fixed target URLs are explicit. This is normal marketplace submission behavior but remains a notable network pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive File Inspection of User-Supplied Directory
dangerous_patterns.description: The audit script reads text files under the supplied skill path and may print matching sensitive lines to standard output.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Directory walking and output formatting are clear in the source. The behavior is local and expected, but it can expose sensitive snippets in logs.

content.value_statement: Publishing a skill can expose private files, broken metadata, or unverified submission paths. This skill guides audit, packaging, GitHub publication, and marketplace submission in one workflow.
actual_capabilities: Runs a local public-safety audit for secrets, local paths, and non-portable references.
Guides when to publish the source skill or build a public package first.
Checks required marketplace metadata such as SKILL.md, support files, and license expectations.
Provides a GitHub publication workflow for a clean public repository or skill subdirectory.
Submits Skillstore requests and sends SkillMap listing requests through documented endpoints.
Records submission IDs, status URLs, timestamps, and manual follow-up items.
limitations: It depends on marketplace endpoints remaining available and accurate after the documented check date.
It cannot guarantee marketplace approval or review speed after submission.
It requires human review before publishing skills with scripts, secrets, or private business logic.
It assumes the user has local GitHub and Python tooling configured when running the full workflow.

use_cases.title: Publish a Personal Skill
use_cases.description: Audit a local Codex or Claude skill, prepare a clean public package, and submit the repository to Skillstore.

use_cases.title: Prepare Team Release Review
use_cases.description: Check a team skill for public-safety issues, metadata gaps, and marketplace readiness before creating a public repository.

use_cases.title: Request Marketplace Listing
use_cases.description: Build a submission note with repository URL, summary, safety status, and contact details for marketplace maintainers.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Skill
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to audit my local skill at <path> for public publishing. Tell me which findings block publication and which are warnings.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Public Package
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to decide whether <path> should be published directly or packaged in public mode. Then list the exact files that need review.

prompt_templates.title: Publish to GitHub and Submit
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to prepare <path> for a public GitHub repo named <owner>/<repo>, then submit it to Skillstore with concise release notes.

prompt_templates.title: Run Multi-Marketplace Release
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to audit, package, publish, and submit <path> to Skillstore and SkillMap. Record IDs, timestamps, URLs, and follow-up actions.

output_examples.input: Audit a local skill before public release.
output: Public-safety audit summary with blocking findings, warnings, and clean checks.
Recommendation to publish source directly or build a public package first.
Short list of files that require manual review before submission.

output_examples.input: Submit a prepared GitHub repository to Skillstore.
output: Submission result with success status, returned submission identifier, and status page URL.
Notes describing the submitted repository URL and any pending marketplace review steps.

output_examples.input: Request a SkillMap listing for a public skill.
output: A concise listing request message with skill name, repository URL, summary, and safety statement.
A final record of the request route and any manual contact follow-up.

best_practices: Run the audit before creating a public repository or sharing a marketplace URL.
Use a public package when the source folder contains local paths, private notes, or owner-only assumptions.
Verify marketplace endpoints and submission requirements on the day you submit.
anti_patterns: Do not publish a source skill that contains secrets, private business logic, or local-only dependencies.
Do not claim support for a marketplace unless its current intake path has been verified.
Do not submit repository URLs or contact details without explicit user approval.

faq.question: Does this skill publish directly to every marketplace?
faq.answer: No. It supports verified routes for Skillstore and a feedback-based SkillMap listing request.

faq.question: Can it detect real secrets before publishing?
faq.answer: It runs a local scanner for common secret patterns, local paths, and private references, but human review is still required.

faq.question: Does it need network access?
faq.answer: Yes, only for submission steps that post the public repository URL and optional notes to marketplace endpoints.

faq.question: Can I publish a skill from a larger repository?
faq.answer: Yes, if the target marketplace accepts a public subdirectory URL and all referenced files are available.

faq.question: What should I do if the audit finds high severity issues?
faq.answer: Stop the publication workflow, remove or sanitize the issue, and rerun the audit before submitting.

faq.question: Is marketplace approval guaranteed?
faq.answer: No. The skill prepares and submits the request, but marketplace maintainers control approval and listing timelines.
